coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic degradation of methyl green dye by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Optimisation study by Taguchi approach
coredata.dc:creator: Abd Rani, Umairah
Ng, Law Yong
Ng, Yee Sern
Ng, Ching Yin
Ong, Ying Hui
Lim, Ying Pei
coredata.dc:description: Empty fruit bunches (EFB) biomass can be a sustainable source for the fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) through hydrothermal treatment. However, the performance and stability of NCQDs produced for methyl green (MG) photocatalysis are yet to be evaluated. This study discusses the photodegradation of MG using NCQDs produced from EFB through the Taguchi approach. At room temperature and an MG dye solution of pH 7, functionalised NCQDs performed as effective photocatalysts in degrading 99% of MG dye under UV-light irradiation (302 nm) within 20 min. NCQDs fabricated from EFB showed outstanding stability and reactivity after 12 months of storage duration. NCQDs could be reused in photocatalysis for at least five cycles while maintaining their photo-degradability of MG above 70%. NCQDs fabricated from EFB biomass show great potential in treating wastewater contaminated with organic dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot
Photoluminescence
Photocatalyst
Organic dye
Taguchi approach

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic degradation of methyl green dye
Zeta potential analysis and functional group determination
Surface morphology and particle size determination
Photocatalytic degradation of methyl green dye
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as photocatalyst
Stability study of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman, Malaysia [ IPSR/RMC/UTARRF/2020-C1/N03 ]; and UCSI University, Malaysia [ REIG-FETBE-2020/066 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Zeta potential measurement and (b) the FTIR spectrum of the fabricated NCQDs.
(a) PL spectrum of NCQDs and (b) the UV–Vis spectrum of NCQDs with an image of the fluorescent NCQDs solution.

(a) Surface morphology of NCQDs observed by SEM at 50x magnification, (b) particle distribution of NCQDs obtained from TEM, (c) a histogram of particle distribution in determining the particle sizes of NCQDs and (d) SAED pattern for the fabricated NCQDs.
X-ray diffraction pattern of the fabricated NCQDs.
Mean of signal-to-noise ratios for four parameters in the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye.
A postulated mechanism for photocatalytic activity by the fabricated NCQDs that were fabricated from empty fruit bunches.
Plots of UV-light absorption spectra for the degradation of MG dye within 30 min.
A plot of t/Ct versus UV-light irradiation time for photocatalytic degradation of MG dye.
The reusability of NCQDs for at least five consecutive cycles.
(a) The degradation rate of MG dye using NCQDs before storage for twelve months and (b) the degradation rate of MG dye using NCQDs that have been stored for twelve months.
Experimental design for four factors and three levels.
Taguchi L9 (34) orthogonal array and results of dye degradation in percentage.
SN ratios for each factors corresponding to its level.
The degradation process of MG dye solutions using the NCQDs as photocatalysts in the presence of scavenger agents.
Photocatalytic degradation of MG dye using CQDs, NCQDs and without catalyst.

row.entry: MG degradation with and without photocatalyst

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic degradation of methyl green dye by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Optimisation study by Taguchi approach

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lee Kong Chian Faculty of Engineering and Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Empty fruit bunches (EFB) biomass can be a sustainable source for the fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) through hydrothermal treatment. However, the performance and stability of NCQDs produced for methyl green (MG) photocatalysis are yet to be evaluated. This study discusses the photodegradation of MG using NCQDs produced from EFB through the Taguchi approach. At room temperature and an MG dye solution of pH 7, functionalised NCQDs performed as effective photocatalysts in degrading 99% of MG dye under UV-light irradiation (302 nm) within 20 min. NCQDs fabricated from EFB showed outstanding stability and reactivity after 12 months of storage duration. NCQDs could be reused in photocatalysis for at least five cycles while maintaining their photo-degradability of MG above 70%. NCQDs fabricated from EFB biomass show great potential in treating wastewater contaminated with organic dyes.

ce:section.ce:para: Empty fruit bunches (EFB) from Elaeis guineensis are one of the significant agricultural wastes in Malaysia, which contains organic materials such as lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose [1]. EFB contains up to 73% fibres and has good biocompatibility with abundant aromatic bio-based macromolecules. The elemental compositions of the EFB fibres consist of several elements such as carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen and oxygen-based functional groups [2], which are beneficial in constructing the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs). By increasing the rate of EFB utilisation, carbon-based photocatalysts could be produced while realising the possibility of zero waste discharge from the palm oil mill industries. Therefore, EFB can be recognised as a high-potential feedstock to be used as a raw material in fabricating NCQDs.

NCQDs can be classified as carbon-based nanoparticles with particle sizes of a few nanometers. They have received attention in various applications owing to their low toxicity, high chemical stability, excellent fluorescence, and outstanding biocompatibility [3,4]. Methyl green (MG) dye is a basic cationic dye-containing triphenylmethane chemical structure, a non-degradable organic dye due to its complex structure [6]. It is widely used for staining solutions in the medicine and textile industries for dyeing cotton and colouring waxes [5]. Despite that, the MG dye can be categorised as a harmful water pollutant because it may affect the oral cavity, leading to the oesophagus perforation, damage to the respiratory tract, and resistance to natural biological degradation. The challenging separation method has limited the direct application of NCQDs as photocatalysts in photodegradation [7–9]. Besides, evaluation of the NCQDs performance alone is essential to ensure an accurate measurement of the photocatalytic behaviour of the NCQDs.

The current study has used the column chromatography method to extract the NCQDs from the solution. Extracted NCQDs can be used repeatedly for at least five cycles while maintaining their photocatalytic behaviour. The photocatalytic behaviour of the NCQDs can be validated by comparing the degraded dyes in the absence and presence of NCQDs. The stability and reactivity of the fabricated NCQDs in this study remained unchanged after twelve months of storage duration.

Recent studies have reported the employment of synthetic material such as citric acid as a raw material in preparing NCQDs via a hydrothermal treatment [10–12]. However, the current work emphasises a sustainable approach by using agricultural wastes as a raw material in fabricating NCQDs. Besides, the photodegradation activity in this study will be evaluated based on the performance of NCQDs without additional oxidising agents. The Taguchi method is suggested and ideal for analysing the process performance by considering the parameter effects and identifying significant variables [13]. The key parameters are essential to creating a good decision with minimum deviations. Therefore, the Taguchi method could identify the photodegradation activity contribution factors.

The characterisation of NCQDs and the photocatalytic kinetic model has been evaluated in this work. In this study, NCQDs were fabricated through a hydrothermal treatment, converting the amorphous solids into a crystalline form. The high temperature and pressure generated during the heating process would disintegrate the bulk molecules into layers of carbon sheets with nano-sized particles. NCQDs have been suggested in the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye due to their fluorescent properties that could efficiently prevent the recombination of photo-excited charge carriers. The surface defect of NCQDs may affect their optical properties and thus significantly enhance the photocatalytic degradation activity.

Empty fruit bunches were collected from Seri Ulu Langat Palm Oil Mill Sdn Bhd, Dengkil, Selangor. Sodium hydroxide pellets (≥97%), methyl green dye (C.l. 42590 ≤ 100%), and hydrochloric acid (38%) were supplied by Merck. N-Hexane (≤100%), urea (≥99%), and silica gel (0.2–1.0 mm) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: 5 g of EFB powders was mixed with 100 mL of deionised water and was heated up to 70 °C for 1 h. Then, 1 g of urea was added to the EFB solution, and the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 8 h. The produced NCQDs solution was in brownish liquid form, and it was stored at 5 °C for further use.

Surface morphology and the particle sizes of NCQDs were identified using scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi S-3400 N) and transmission electron microscopy (Thermo Fisher; Talos 120C). The zeta potential analysis was carried out using dynamic light scattering (Malvern) at room temperature with distilled water at pH 7 as the aqueous medium. The light absorption analysis on the NCQDs solution was observed using a double-beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-260) at the absorption wavelength range of 200 nm to 800 nm. X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) (model Shimadzu XRD-6000) was used in analysing the crystallinity of NCQDs in which the scanning range was between 0° to 80°.

The functional groups on the NCQD surfaces were identified by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Nicolet iS10). The spectral width was observed from 4000 to 400 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1, and the sample was recorded at 32 scans. The luminescent properties of NCQDs were evaluated using photoluminescence spectroscopy (Edinburgh Instrument FLS920) at a detection range of 200 nm to 1000 nm. The total organic carbon of the degraded MG dyes was determined using the Total Organic Carbon (TOC) Analyzer (Shimadzu, SSM-5000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of methyl green dye
ce:section.ce:para:
The mixture of dye and NCQDs solution at constant concentration was exposed to the UV-light irradiation at a wavelength of 302 nm at room temperature at the specified duration using Nyxtechnik UV box T12621D as a UV-light irradiation source. The MG dye concentration in the solutions was analysed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 500 – 700 nm after the photocatalytic process. The degradation efficiency for MG dye can be calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
D e g r a d a t i o n
r a t e
%
=
C
0
-
C
t
/
C
0
× 100 %
where C0 represents the initial MG dye concentration, and Ct represents the MG dye concentration at a specific time. The effect of operating conditions on the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye was evaluated using the Taguchi approach, a method based on the statistical principle. This method would lead to a considerable decrease in the number of experiments for modelling the response functions. In this study, the Taguchi orthogonal array (OA) of the factorial design was employed using Minitab. In this study, four parameters were investigated, namely amount of NCQDs (A), pH of dye solution (B), duration of degradation (C), and initial dye concentration (D). There are three levels for each parameter, as shown in Table 1 .

Based on the parameters combinations, the Taguchi method has suggested that an L9 orthogonal array design [14] is sufficient to systematically predict the significant variables and the effect of each factor on the degradation efficiency using nine sets of experiments. The arrangement of the experiments is shown in Table 2 .

The analysis using the Taguchi method was evaluated using the Signal to Noise (SN) ratio. SN ratio is a method to identify the optimum level of each factor by measuring the mean values (represents the desirable effect) and the standard deviation (represents the undesirable effect) of the data set [15]. It can be classified into three categories: the larger-is-better, the nominal-is-better, and the smaller-is-better. In this study, the degradation efficiency was the desirable primary response. Therefore, the SN ratio chosen in the Taguchi method was the larger-is-better, and it can be calculated using Eq. (2).
(2)
S N = - 10 l o g 10
[
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
1
yi
2
]
where n is the number of experiments and yi is the degradation efficiency.

A kinetic study for the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye was carried out using seven bottles of 100 mL MG dye solution at a concentration of 20 ppm and pH 7. 10 mL of NCQDs was added into each bottle. The mixtures were exposed to UV-light irradiation for the time interval of 0, 5, 10, 15, 20 and 30 min. After the irradiation, the sample bottle was taken out and analysed for MG dye concentration in the solution using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The results were fitted into pseudo-second-order kinetics models, as shown in Eq. (3) [16]:
(3)
d
C
t
/ d t = k
C
e
-
C
t
2
where Ce and Ct are the concentration of degraded MG dye at the equilibrium and specific time, respectively, and k is the rate constant. The integrated equation can be expressed in Eq. (4), in which the rate constant of MG degradation can be obtained from the intercept of the linear-line plots of t/Ct versus t.
(4)
t /
C t
= 1 /
(
k
C e
2
)
+ t /
C e

NCQDs were used as photocatalysts in liquid form. After the degradation activity, NCQDs were separated from the dye solution for reuse in the subsequent cycles. In this study, column chromatography was applied to re-collect the NCQDs from the dye solution, in which n-hexane and silica gel were used as the mobile and stationary phases, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential analysis and functional group determination
ce:section.ce:para: The zeta potential measurement shows that NCQDs exhibit a negative charge in the solution with a conductivity of −19.5 mV, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The negatively charged surfaces may generate a strong electrostatic force to attract cationic components for photocatalytic degradation, in this case, MG dye. Moreover, negative zeta potential also signified that the electrostatic forces are repulsive between the molecules. Repulsion between the molecules suggested that the application of NCQDs should be more stable in the dispersed system. The negative charges of NCQDs indicated their good stability in an aqueous solution. NCQDs maintained good stability by storing at 5 °C for twelve months. The negative charge also indicated the presence of oxygenated-functional groups on the surface of NCQDs, which is proven by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum, as shown in Fig. 1b.

The presence of various functional groups on the NCQD surfaces was confirmed by four significant bands at 3325.10 cm−1, 2924.35 cm−1, 2925.22 cm−1 and 1616.24 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH, CH, NH and CO groups, respectively.

Another two absorption bands were observed at 1340.15 cm−1 and 1043.05 cm−1, attributed to CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The formation of aromatic rings on the NCQD surface was demonstrated by CC groups at 1415.23 cm−1. The presence of oxygenated-functional groups has made NCQDs a hydrophilic material, resulting in high solubility in an aqueous medium [17]. Different functional groups on the NCQDs surface will create different emissive traps during the excitation of electrons, resulting in NCQDs with good photoluminescent properties [18]. Various functional groups on the NCQD surfaces can create new energy levels that cause highly luminescent and excellent optical properties. The abundant surface defects contributed by the functional groups can enhance the long-term stability of NCQDs. These synergistic effects can contribute to the high performance of photocatalytic degradation of methyl green dye. The presence of functional groups on the NCQD surfaces could be created by surface defects, which is vital in increasing the absorption of UV-light irradiation during the photodegradation activity.

Fig. 2 a shows the luminescent properties of NCQDs obtained from photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, which showed the excitation and emission wavelengths at 415 nm and 559 nm, respectively. The fluorescent intensity of NCQDs is strongly dependent on the corresponding excitation wavelength. The corresponding excitation wavelength can be related to the transition of electrons from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital to the π orbital transition of carbon sheets [19].

The photoluminescence property of NCQDs can be attributed to the presence of sp2 and sp3 carbon core with quantum confinement and the presence of different trap states on the surfaces of NCQDs. The functional surface groups of NCQDs have promoted the trap states with different energy levels between the π and π* states of carbon–carbon double structures, resulting in excitation and emission of photons.

A UV–Vis spectrophotometer can be used to observe light absorption and the optical properties of NCQDs. Fig. 2b shows the UV–Vis spectrum of NCQDs produced from EFB fibres. A significant absorption band can be seen in the range of 220–280 nm, which could be explained by the π-π* electronic transition of the conjugated system containing the aromatic π system [20]. Another absorption peak at 350 nm could be attributed to the transition of n-π*, related to the CO bonds. The UV–Vis spectrum of NCQDs obtained in this work was coincidently similar to a previous study [21].

Besides, the NCQD solution emits a strong blue fluorescence when exposed to a UV lamp (Fig. 2b (embedded image)), which could signify that the NCQDs produced in this work have more surface defects, which caused more electrons to radiate in the form of photons [22]. In addition, the well-dispersed aqueous suspension of NCQDs also enhances the emission of blue fluorescence once exposed to UV-light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and particle size determination
ce:section.ce:para: Surface morphologies of the dried NCQDs were observed by the scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fig. 3 a shows the surface morphology image of NCQD particles, in which the particles showed uniform distribution and spherical shape overall.

The images showed NCQDs with slightly uneven shapes, indicating that the fabricated NCQD particle surface combines amorphous and crystalline structures [7]. The smaller particle sizes of the NCQDs led to the formation of stronger inter-particle attractions, which provided a large functional surface area that favoured their functionality as an effective photocatalyst in degrading organic dyes such as methyl green dye. The particle size distribution and the fabricated NCQDs were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the result is shown in Fig. 3b.

The size distribution of NCQDs was relatively narrow, and the shapes of the particle were relatively uniform. The shape of NCQDs is spherical with several nanometers in diameter, proving the successful formation of quantum dots through hydrothermal treatment. Fig. 3c shows a histogram of NCQDs particles diameter versus the area of particle size distribution calculated using mini-tab software. The particle size distribution was calculated by measuring approximately 100 particles of NCQDs.

The histogram indicated that the distribution of NCQD particle sizes ranged from 2.0 to 4.5 nm, and their average diameter was found to be 3.2 nm. The particle sizes of NCQDs can be categorised as nano-sized, which were in quantum dot ranges and in good agreement with a previously reported work [23]. When the obtained NCQDs particle size is at the nanoscale, a high particle surface area will give higher sensitivity to UV-light irradiation. Hence, light photons can be significantly absorbed by NCQD particles, improving the photodegradation performance.

NCQDs could provide a large surface area to reduce the photo-charge carrier recombination rate and facilitate the migration of electrons, which is favourable for photocatalytic degradation of methyl green dye [24]. In addition, the blazing spots and the diffuse rings in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern revealed the crystalline nature of NCQDs [25], as shown in Fig. 3d.

The crystallinity of NCQDs is significant for photocatalytic degradation of MG dye through the photo-generated electron and holes, enhancing the electrons transfer rate during photocatalytic degradation activity [26].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was employed to investigate the crystalline phases of NCQD surfaces. As shown in Fig. 4 , the XRD pattern demonstrated a sharp and high-intensity reflection centred at 27.8° that corresponded to the characteristic inter-planar stacking structure of the conjugated aromatic systems, which are indexed for carbon-based materials [27]. The diffraction pattern of the NCQDs shows peaks centred at around 22.5° and 23.7°, respectively, which were related to the amorphous carbon, indicating a highly crystalline nature and high purity of NCQDs.

Based on Fig. 4, the high-intensity peaks at around 20° to 30° suggested the presence of an amorphous carbon phase in the fabricated NCQD surfaces, ascribing to a slightly disordered carbon atom [28]. A strong peak was observed at around 35°, characteristic of (010) diffractions from the graphite phase due to the agglomeration of NCQDs during the freeze-drying process.

It can be concluded that NCQDs fabricated from empty fruit bunches have a crucial role in forming composite nanostructure to obtain a more efficient electron pathway. The crystallinity of NCQDs fabricated from EFB fibres showed excellent interfacial charge transfer, improved molecular oxygen activation ability, and increased light-harvesting capacity to enhance the photocatalytic degradation activity [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of methyl green dye
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 illustrates the SN ratio for different operating parameters. In general, it was found that the increase of solution pH and initial dye concentration improved the SN ratio of the system, indicating that higher degradation efficiency could be achieved. In contrast, the increase of NCQDs amount and duration only improved the system's performance up to an optimum point, in this case, 10 mL and 20 min, respectively. Among the parameters, pH of dye solution has the highest impact on the system's performance, followed by dye concentration, NCQDs amount and duration of photocatalysis, as shown in the delta analysis of SN ratio in Table 3 .

Fig. 5 shows that the dye solution could be degraded most efficiently in an alkaline medium due to a high number of hydroxyl molecules. When NCQDs and sodium hydroxide solutions were added to the dye solution and then exposing the medium to UV-light irradiation, electrons and holes activated by NCQDs could react with hydroxyl molecules and dissolved molecular oxygen to generate reactive hydroxyl radicals [30,31]. Thus, the MG dye solution could be degraded. The successful degradation of MG dye with the addition of sodium hydroxide solution indicated that the high pH values favour the formation of hydroxyl ions, and there is less competition in the contact between MG dye and NCQD molecules. Therefore, the percentage of MG dye degradation was higher in the solution with higher pH (pH 7 – 9).

In contrast, the degradation activity was negligible in the acidic medium, which could be due to the suppression in forming the hydroxyl radicals in acidic solution as the concentration of proton ions was higher than the hydroxyl ions. In addition, the degradation process was prolonged, probably due to the repulsion between NCQDs and cationic dye molecules, owing to the excess positive charges.

Solution at low pH conditions disrupted the NCQD surface hydroxyl groups due to the protonation process that hindered the photodegradation process. The fluorescent properties of NCQDs are slightly weaker in acidic solutions due to the deprotonation of oxygen-containing functional groups [32]. The deprotonated oxygen-containing functional groups caused lower energy to be released, which eventually decreased the photocatalytic degradation activity. The degradation activity can be maintained by photocatalysis in a neutral dye solution or at pH 7 by controlling the duration of degradation and the NCQDs amount, as reported in previous study [33].

Photocatalytic degradation of MG dye suggested by the Taguchi approach was carried out at pH 7 of dye solution and showed a high photodegradation rate. Apart from pH for dye solution, Fig. 5 also shows that the degradation activity became faster when the initial dye concentration was increased. The higher photodegradation activity at a higher initial dye concentration was mainly due to a higher degree of attraction and reaction between NCQDs and dye molecules. The effective contacts between the oxidising species and dye molecules were enhanced, resulting in higher degradation efficiency.

In contrast, the study revealed that the required amount of NCQDs was 10 mL at a concentration of 10 ppm for every 100 mL of dye solution. 10 mL of NCQDs can provide sufficient active sites for generating a higher number of radicals. Nevertheless, it is noteworthy that beyond this dosage, the suspension's turbidity in the mixture caused by the NCQDs could decrease UV-light penetration. The higher suspension's turbidity could reduce the UV-light irradiation from reaching the particle surfaces and cause lower degradation efficiency. Lastly, the duration for dye degradation activity was the least significant according to the delta analysis ranking in Table 3.

The dye degradation duration was usually highly dependent on the initial dye concentration and the amount of NCQDs used in photocatalysis. Nevertheless, the results showed that the SN ratio for MG dye degradation was preferable in 20 min. The degradation performance, in general, showed that the degradation efficiency was stable at 60–70% for the degradation duration between 10 and 30 min. Based on the results as shown in Fig. 5, the desirable levels of the parameters were concluded at: dye solution pH of 7 to 9, initial dye concentration of 30 ppm, NCQDs amount of 10 mL, and the duration of degradation of 20 min. Two sets of confirmation runs have been conducted, and it was found that an average value of 99.2% MG dye degradation could be obtained from this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: NCQDs fabricated from empty fruit bunches in this study could be a good photocatalyst in the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye. NCQDs could act as an electron reservoir to trap the photo-generated electrons and promote the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The nano-sized NCQDs possess a large surface area with plenty of active surfaces, improving UV-light absorption and contributing to higher redox reactions [34,35].

High electron mobility can occur in NCQDs, which improves the charge transferability and suppresses the electron-hole recombination rate. In addition, the fluorescent NCQDs that were fabricated from empty fruit bunches possess 3.2 nm in particle sizes, indicating their high capability in emitting blue fluorescence when exposed under UV-light irradiation.

The blue fluorescence of NCQDs can be categorised as the smallest quantum dots in particle size with a larger band-gap, reducing the possibility of radiation recombination. This result can produce a high number of electron and hole pairs, thereby improving the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye in a more sustainable reaction. When NCQDs are exposed to UV-light irradiation, electrons in NCQDs are excited into higher energy states, producing photo-generated charge carriers. Hence, the oxidation and reduction processes can be performed to generate active radicals.

The photocatalytic mechanism by the NCQDs fabricated from empty fruit bunches is postulated as shown in Fig. 6 . The generated active radicals, such as hydroxyl and superoxide radicals, are chemically reactive and highly unstable in their radical forms. These radicals could effectively degrade the MG dye molecules without other agents. Based on the postulated mechanism, the free radicals stimulated by the NCQD photocatalysts could contribute to the primary degradation activity. The final products of the degradation activity could be carbon dioxide and water molecules.

In the absence of NCQDs, MG dye molecules were stable without oxidation–reduction reactions. The excited electrons and oxidising free radicals would not be generated in the photocatalytic system. Therefore, the degradation activity was relatively slow. When NCQDs are added to the MG dye solution, the excited electrons will further shuttle through the NCQDs structural framework. Therefore, the charge separation process and the generation of active radicals could be performed [36], bringing about a higher possibility of degrading MG dye, leading to remarkable photodegradation activity.

The NCQDs fabricated from empty fruit bunches showed the lowest PL intensity of 530 nm, suggesting an extremely low recombination rate of photo-generated charge carriers [37]. The low recombination rate of electron and hole pairs could contribute to the effective photodegradation process. NCQDs are luminescent materials that can improve the UV-light utilisation properties. The excellent electronic conductivity of NCQDs can enlarge the photon absorption region and improve the UV-light absorption intensity.

Under UV-light irradiation, NCQDs were conducive to providing many active sites and shortening the paths of electron transportation. Hence, NCQDs produced in this study can significantly promote the charge separation process during photocatalytic degradation activity on the MG dye. Therefore, the intrinsic absorption ranges of UV-light were extended, increasing the photocatalytic activity of NCQDs [38]. Besides, the generated holes can directly react with hydroxyl ions to generate hydroxyl radicals, acting as strong oxidising agents in the photocatalytic degradation activity [39].

The presence of various oxygenated-functional groups on the NCQD surfaces can cause band bending, which provides an internal electric field that induces the separation of electron-hole pairs, thus, improving photocatalytic activity [40]. The defect surface of NCQDs has expanded the UV-light utilisation and provided more charge carriers in redox reactions. This result significantly improved the photocatalytic degradation activity of the MG dye.

In order to verify the heterojunction type in photocatalytic degradation activity, the contribution of reactive oxygen species was evaluated to determine the radicals that contributed to the photocatalytic degradation activity. Hydroxyl radical (•OH) and superoxide radical (•O2) are the dominant reactive oxygen species in most photocatalytic processes [8].

Trapping experiments were carried out using isopropyl alcohol and benzoquinone as scavenging agents to confirm the roles of hydroxyl and superoxide radicals in the photocatalysis of MG in this study. A 0.05 mol of isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and 0.05 mol of benzoquinone (BQ) were added to 20 ppm of dye solution (100 mL). The scavenging agents were used to trap •OH and •O2 radicals, respectively, during the photocatalytic degradation activity under UV-light irradiation. The condition of the experiment was conducted as suggested by the Taguchi approach.

Table 4 shows the effect of the scavengers on the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes in the presence of NCQDs as photocatalysts. The result showed the dye degradation efficiency became lower after the addition of scavenger agents. The IPA and BQ were the primary inhibitors in the degradation process for MG dye.

Theoretically, scavenger agents may react with radicals generated during the photodegradation process, decreasing the number of free radicals [41]. Thus, in the presence of scavenger agents, photocatalytic degradation became ineffective. The presence of scavenger agents could also inhibit the light penetration on the NCQD surfaces during the degradation activity.

This study indicated that the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye solution using NCQDs was mainly based on the oxidative activity of hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals. Moreover, NCQDs possess highly negative charges in the solution as the pH value of NCQDs solution was 9.3. Therefore, there is a high tendency to generate reactive radicals once exposed to UV-light irradiation. The current study suggests that NCQDs are excellent fluorescent photocatalysts in the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye.

Comparative experiments were carried out to evaluate the performance of NCQDs as a photocatalyst in degrading MG dye solution. The photocatalytic degradation activity in this work was carried out using the dye solution at pH 7. In the presence of NCQDs, about 99% of MG dye solution could be degraded in 30 min of UV-light irradiation. Fig. 7 displays plots of UV-light absorption spectra for the degradation of MG dye within 30 min.

On the contrary, in the absence of NCQDs, the degradation percentages were below 40 %, as indicated in Table 5 . Results suggest that the NCQDs could be an effective photocatalyst in degrading MG dye. NCQDs are constructed by aromatic systems in a layered carbon-based structure, which could provide stronger π-π intermolecular interactions, producing strong fluorescent NCQDs capable of generating radicals to degrade the MG molecules in the presence of a light source. Data in Table 5 shows the effectiveness of NCQDs in degrading the MG dye molecules.

Results indicated that the NCQDs showed the highest percentage of degradation rate (99.91%) compared to those in the presence of CQDs (39.27%) and without any catalyst (21.35%). NCQDs possess abundant defects in their surfaces, providing multiple reflections of UV-light irradiation and acting as good photo-sensitiser. The defect-rich surface expanded the UV-light utilisation and provided more charge carriers in redox reactions, significantly improving the photocatalytic degradation activity. The presence of nitrogen elements on the CQD surfaces improved the optical properties of NCQDs, which resulted in a high concentration of electronic density and enhanced the electron's migration during photodegradation activity.

NCQDs showed better performance than CQDs due to the different optical and fluorescence properties. The doping element corresponds to a relatively wide distribution of different energy levels to generate a broad UV-light absorption band. The electronegativity of the NCQD structure in the presence of nitrogen elements could delocalise electrons, leading to an electron-rich condition. The delocalisation of the conjugated π structures can induce rapid separation and accelerate the movement of photo-generated charges, leading to a more effective photocatalytic degradation performance than CQDs.

In addition, the application of urea during the fabrication of NCQDs could increase the number of oxygen atoms through surface oxidation, which primarily served as a capture centre for excitons, thus giving rise to surface-state-related fluorescence. As the density of the nitrogen element increases, the quantum yield of NCQDs could be improved when more electrons could be trapped by the new surface states, leading to a high generation of free radicals. These properties could make the NCQDs more effective in degrading organic dyes than CQDs.

Total organic carbon (TOC) can be an appropriate parameter to measure organic carbon concentration in the degraded dye solution. The lower value of TOC indicates a better performance of NCQDs in the degradation of MG dye solution. Generally, the degraded dye solution consists of water and carbon dioxide molecules [42]. However, there is a high possibility that the degraded dye solutions contain other eliminated products, which can be estimated using the TOC analysis. Based on the TOC result, it was observed that after 30 min of photocatalytic degradation activity, the TOC value of the degraded MG dye solution decreased to 7.92 mg/L.

The total organic carbon concentration in the degraded MG dye solution was low, indicating that the degraded MG dye solution contains sufficient oxygen molecules. This result suggested the successful mineralisation of MG dye solution by NCQDs to carbon dioxide and water molecules. Thus, the fabricated NCQDs from empty fruit bunches exhibited desirable mineralisation ability towards MG dye solution. The mineralisation ability of the NCQDs shows the potential for wastewater treatment, especially when dealing with organic pollutants.

As illustrated in Fig. 8 , the kinetic study shows a linear plot of t/Ct versus irradiation time with a correlation coefficient of 0.9934. The rate constant of photocatalytic degradation activity was 3.3386 ppm/min. The linear plot with a high correlation coefficient suggested that the pseudo-second-order model can describe the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye by NCQDs. The photodegradation reaction might occur at the active sites of the NCQDs on their surfaces [42].

The negative surface charge of NCQDs favours the electrostatic interaction with the positively charged MG dye. When the solution of MG dye is exposed to UV-light irradiation, the NCQD particles become active in releasing electrons for excitation. More free radicals can then be generated, leading to better degradation of the MG dye molecules.

In this study, the high kinetic rate in MG photodegradation can be explained by nano-sized NCQDs, which possess large surface area, high conductivity, and high capability in electron capture. NCQDs can break down the dye molecules due to ionic mobility, while the high kinetic rate indicates an enhanced ionic movement to convert them into less toxic components [43]. The results indicated that the NCQDs fabricated from empty fruit bunches are significant photocatalysts in the photodegradation of MG dye molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability study of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of NCQDs was evaluated based on their reusability. Fig. 9 shows that 99% MG dye was degraded in the first cycle of NCQDs application. The degradation efficiency was slightly decreased in the subsequent cycles, which might be attributed to the surface oxidation of NCQDs that could reduce the number of reactive sites on the NCQD surfaces [44].

In addition, the decrease in degradation efficiency in the subsequent cycle of photocatalysis could be explained by the loss of NCQDs during the separation of NCQDs from the solution by column chromatography. Nevertheless, it is noteworthy that the degradation efficiency remained above 70% for the consecutive cycles, indicating that the fabricated NCQDs possessed good reactivity and stability. The hydrophilic property of the polar functional groups on the NCQD surfaces enhanced the stability of NCQDs in aqueous systems, which could improve the electron transfer capabilities during the photodegradation activity [45].

In addition to maintaining high stability in photocatalytic behaviour during the operation, the fabricated NCQDs from empty fruit bunches were stable when stored for twelve months in a 5 °C refrigerator due to their strong chemical bonds and colloidal stability in distilled water.

The stability of the NCQDs in deionised water could be explained by the functional groups present in their structures. The NCQDs can form intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds with water molecules, thereby enhancing the hydrophilicity properties and preventing the corruption of chemical structure [46]. Since the fabricated NCQDs were prepared from agricultural waste and without toxic chemicals during the fabrication process, they are non-toxic in characteristics and convenient for long-term storage.

Fig. 10 a shows the degradation efficiency of MG dye using the fabricated NCQDs before storage for twelve months. In order to evaluate the stability and reactivity of the fabricated NCQDs, they have been used as a photocatalyst in the degradation of MG dye after twelve months of storage. Fig. 10b shows the degradation efficiency of MG dye using the stored NCQDs. The results showed that the NCQDs maintained their fluorescent properties after being stored for twelve months, suggesting a consistent efficiency after the long storage duration.

The excellent stability could also be attributed to the π-π stacking interactions between the NCQD sheets, which exhibited significant tolerance against photo-bleaching under continuous UV-light irradiation. The fluorescence properties of NCQDs are also stable due to the lone pairs of electrons contributed by nitrogen atoms, which can serve as electron donors to supply electrons to the π-conjugated system of carbon. Thus, the surface electrons and diffusion channels of the NCQDs structure were improved, contributing to the high stability of NCQDs in the solution.

Nitrogen doping can efficiently induce charge delocalisation and tuning of the work function of carbon, which effectively changes the electronic characteristics and energy states of NCQDs. Moreover, the electron transfer capability of carbon materials can be efficiently promoted to enhance the desirable photocatalytic performance in degrading the MG dyes through the fabrication of NCQDs from empty fruit bunches.

In this study, NCQDs were fabricated successfully using empty fruit bunches through a hydrothermal and nitrogen-doping procedure. The average particle size of NCQDs was found to be 3.2 nm. From the Taguchi study, NCQDs fabricated from the empty fruit bunches showed excellent performance in the photocatalytic degradation of MG dyes. They can degrade the MG dyes up to 99% within 20 min of UV-light irradiation. The percentages of MG dye degradation were higher in the solution with an increasing solution pH (pH 7 – 9). The photodegradation process follows a pseudo-second-order kinetic model, with a 3.3386 ppm/min rate constant. NCQDs from this study were recovered using column chromatography and reused for at least five consecutive reaction runs while maintaining their photodegradation capability towards the MG dyes at above 70%. NCQDs fabricated from empty fruit bunches also displayed excellent stability in photocatalytic performance in degrading the MG molecules after being stored for 12 months at 5 °C. The excellent performance of the NCQDs fabricated from empty fruit bunches offers a new alternative to producing a highly efficient and non-toxic photocatalyst that could decompose a wide range of organic pollutants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman, Malaysia [IPSR/RMC/UTARRF/2020-C1/N03]; and UCSI University, Malaysia [REIG-FETBE-2020/066].
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.
